moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn to e4, immediately staking our claim to the center and freeing both our light-squared bishop and our queen for potential development. This classic first move controls the important d5 and f5 squares, preventing Black from easily establishing pieces there. Our e4 pawn also opens diagonal lines that will become important as we develop our pieces in the coming moves. This move sets up numerous opening possibilities and puts immediate pressure on Black to respond to our central presence.

Our opponent chooses the Caro-Kann Defense with c6, a solid and slightly passive opening that prepares to challenge our e4 pawn with d5 on the next move. This move supports the advance d7-d5 while keeping Black's position compact and hard to attack. Unlike 1...e5 which leads to open, tactical games, the Caro-Kann typically results in more positional struggles where Black aims for a solid pawn structure and long-term piece activity. We should expect Black to play d5 next, directly challenging our central pawn and forcing us to make a key decision about how to maintain our space advantage.

We're developing our light-squared bishop to c4 in what's known as the Hillbilly Attack, an unconventional and somewhat provocative continuation against the Caro-Kann. This move targets the f7 square immediately, which is often a weak point in Black's position since it's only defended by the king. Our bishop on c4 also eyes the long diagonal and can potentially support a future f2-f3 and e4-e5 pawn advance, or participate in tactics involving the f7 weakness. While this isn't the most theoretically sound approach against the Caro-Kann (most masters prefer 2.d4 or 2.Nc3), it can lead to sharp, unbalanced positions where tactical awareness becomes more important than deep theoretical knowledge.
